A10.cmt_0: Neither policy nor standard explicitly spell out key lengths or protocols; some approved algorithms are mentioned. Unlikely that policy is consistently applied in practice.
A10.cmt_1: Formal policy and standard exists but no detailed technical standards documented (i.e key size, length, rotation, retention, storage and other lifecycle properties) or consistently applied. Recommend to formally enforce as a minimum secure architecture baseline pattern for all designs with data classification = > confidential must be encrypted to tech standards at rest, in transport, in processing. Ongoing risk assessment.
A10.cmt_2: Risk based TLS use not universal. All in transit sensitive data protected using TLS 1.2 and above; TLS 1.2+ is min baseline standard. Verification of low level TLS 1.0 explicit disablement and strong cipher suite use needed (discussion w/VMO gov).
A10.cmt_7: Quantum risk is considered in IT Strat Plan. Post quantum cryptography readiness discovery adhoc/under review. Certificate, Secrets discovery and risk assessment needed.

A13.cmt_0: Agree. Ongoing effort to optimize network control automations with real time threat intelligence, firewall rule base cleanup (fw hygiene + hardening).
A13.cmt_1: Agree. Security characteristics formally defined for all network services in security arch design. SLAs defined and enforced with all MSSP services (SOC L1/L2, Security Infra, Security Cyber scan, Security ZTNA). Elevating to 3.5 as service performance metrics review daily, weekly, monthly.
A13.cmt_2: Network segregation and information-flow controls do currently enforce trust boundaries. Network communications with external networks and other interconnected systems are routed only through managed interfaces protected by boundary security controls (e.g., firewalls, gateways/routers, and intrusion detection). Publicly accessible system components are placed in subnetworks that are physically and/or logically separated from internal networks based on risk assessment, and information flows are monitored, inspected, and logged to detect and prevent unauthorized transfers. Factory segmentation in process for 10 factories.
A13.cmt_3: Enforced by NGFW (cloud, on prem) with advanced URL filtering controls to block, override, alert, and allow.
A13.cmt_4: SPF is published and DKIM is enforced. We also operate email DLP using native Microsoft 365 Purview DLP and Mimecast email DLP. These controls prevent unauthorized disclosure of sensitive information via email and operate as complementary layers within a defense‑in‑depth architecture. Mimecast inspects inbound email for impersonation, spoofing, and phishing indicators. M365 enforces anti phishing through MS Defender for Office 365 inside Exchange Online. Together they provide layered detection and prevention of phishing, impersonation, and business email compromise threats.
A13.cmt_5: We segregate OT networks from Corporate IT networks; all connectivity is mediated and enforced through centrally governed firewall controls. This segregation is implemented through documented policies, technical enforcement mechanisms, and ongoing segmentation improvements aligned to ISO/IEC 27001 and industry OT security best practices. OT/IT network segregation is a formally approved, documented security requirement captured in Ops and Comms Security Policies.
A13.cmt_6: Agree w/current disposition. All four controls are (in)formally documented, approved, and enforced within onsemi’s security standards. Technical enforcement of AAA through CISCO ISE. No rogue AP detection and possible WPA2-PSK legacy in use.
A13.cmt_7: Contract/agreements include data protection/handling clauses. Verification required by legal; may be difficult to assess total gaps/exceptions for data exchange agreements.

A14.cmt_0: Information security is integrated across the full IT project lifecycle with mandatory risk assessment at early project stages (IRQ/BCA). ARC serves as a formal security review and approval gate, Go‑Live TAC provides a mandatory final security sign‑off. Projects cannot progress or deploy without satisfying security requirements with exception process management in place for outliers.
A14.cmt_1: SDLC gaps assessed/CMM assessed against SAMM. Program defined and roadmap in process with enforcement progressing across dev teams. Dev training curriculum defined and delivery ongoing.
A14.cmt_3: Yes. The principles are formally documented and operationalized and enforced through ARC 2.0/ECS Design governance, mandatory Security Design Assessments, approved architecture standards, and lifecycle gating. The secure design work performed through ARC ensures these principles are reviewed, documented, and applied before systems are implemented or deployed.
A14.cmt_4: Secure coding standards are now formally defined and enforced through the onsemi Application Security Program. SAST, SCA, DAST integrated into CI/CD pipelines as a security gate is progressing across dev projects, with findings tracked and remediated. Developers receive structured training aligned to OWASP Top 10 principles, reinforced through an ongoing Security Champions program. These activities are operationally led through the AppSec function.
A14.cmt_5: Agree w/current disposition. Progressing but not yet a broad implementation of practices.
A14.cmt_6: Agree w/current disposition. Opportunity for annual review with legal for additional technical security obligations in contracts beyond just NDA.
A14.cmt_7: Agree w/current disposition. Opportunity to integrate segregation policy through Design process. Attempts made to separate but not comprehensive.

A15.cmt_5: Yes. Suppliers are subject to the same security controls and access governance as onsemi personnel. The implementation of Global Protect VPN and Prisma Access has further strengthened security posture by enhancing secure access controls and monitoring.

A15.cmt_7: They go through information security assessment but no in depth assessment for counterfeit, tampered, or compromised parts before deployment.
